coredata.dc:title: Comparison between electrochemical and photoelectrochemical detection of dopamine based on titania-ceria-graphene quantum dots nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmadi, Nasrin
Bagherzadeh, Mojtaba
Nemati, Ali
coredata.dc:description: In this study, titania-ceria-graphene quantum dot (TC-GQD) nanocomposite was synthesized by hydrothermal method for the first time. The prepared nanomaterials were characterized by XRD, FTIR dynamic light scattering (DLS), FESEM, HRTEM, and EDX spectroscopy along with elemental mapping. The synergistic effect of the nanocomposite components was studied by diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) and electrical conductivity meter. The results showed that band gap of TC-GQD nanocomposite was shifted to visible lights relative to its components (1.3 eV), and electrical conductivity of the sample was significant increased to 89.5 μS cm−1. After chemical and physical characterization, prepared new nanocomposites were used to design a new electrochemical (EC) and photoelectrochemical (PEC) dopamine (DA) sensors. In both EC and PEC methods effecting experimental parameters were optimized. Due to the synergic effect of the nanocomposite components, an outstanding photocurrent response was observed for DA based on PEC sensor. A linear calibration curve with a lower detection limit of 22 nM DA, and sensitivity of 13.8 mA/mM(DA), in a wider range of 0.3–750 μM DA, was obtained for TC-GQD/GCE electrode in PEC. While, the TC-GQD/GCE electrode detected DA in the range of 1–500 μM DA, with two linear calibration curve, detection limit of 0.22 μM DA, and sensitivity of 4.9 mA/mM(DA), in the EC. Observed results from EC and PEC sensors are presented and compared.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Synergistic effect
Photoelectrochemical sensor
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of titania-ceria-graphene quantum dot (TC-GQD) nanocomposite
Fabrication of the EC and PEC electrodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a and b) The XRD patterns, c) FTIR, d) DLS, e) DRS, f) Kubelka-Munk curves, and g) PL of GQD, TC and TC-GQD nanocomposites.

a and b) The FESEM images, c) EDX spectrum and elemental composition, d) EDX map analysis, e-g) HRTEM images of TC-GQD nanocomposites. The inset in (b) is the histogram of size distribution of TC-GQD nanoparticles. The scale bares in FESEM images are 500 and 200 nm in a and b, respectively.

Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) of a) GCE, b) GQD/GCE, c) TC/GCE, and d) TC-GQD/GCE in 0.1 M PBS containing 1 mM AA, 100 μM UA with different concentration of DA (0, 5, 25 and 100 μM). e) Schematic represent the EC electron transfer mechanism at TC-GQD/GCE in the presence of DA.

a) Differential pulse voltammograms of TC-GQD/GCE in 0.1 M PBS and 1 mM DA at various pH (3, 4, 5, 6, 7 and 8). b) Cyclic voltammograms of TC-GQD/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH: 4) and 1 mM DA at various scan rates (10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350 and 400 mV/s). c) Variation of the redox peak currents in Fig. 4b against square root of scan rates. d) The DPVs of TC-GQD/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH: 4) containing 1 mM AA, 100 μM UA, and different concentrations of DA)0, 0.3, 1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 150, 250, 400, 500, 750 and 1000 μM(; Insets: the calibration curves for low and high level concentrations of DA, (n = 5).

a) The photocurrent responses of the TC-GQD/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH: 7.4) (1) without and 2) with 50 μM DA. b) The photocurrent responses of GCE, T/GCE, TC/GCE, GQD/GCE, and TC-GQD/GCE electrodes in 0.1 PBS (pH: 7.4) containing 50 μM DA. c) Schematic represent the PEC electron transfer mechanism at TC-GQD/GCE in the presence of DA.

a) The PEC response of the TC-GQD/GCE electrode in 0.1 M PBS containing 50 μM DA at various pH (3, 4, 5, 6, 7 and 8), applied potential as 0.0 V vs. OCP. b) The PEC response of the TC-GQD/GCE electrode in 0.1 M PBS (pH: 7.4) containing 50 μM DA at various applied potential (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4 and 0.5 V). c) The PEC intensity of TC-GQD/GCE electrode in 0.1 M PBS (pH: 7.4) containing the different concentrations of DA: 0, 0.3, 1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 150, 250, 400, 500, 750 and 1000 μM at 0.0 V vs. OCP; Inset: show the linear calibration curve, n = 5.
Determination of DA in pharmaceutical samples.
The analytical performance for DA detection by the EC and PEC methods.

head.ce:title: Comparison between electrochemical and photoelectrochemical detection of dopamine based on titania-ceria-graphene quantum dots nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Engineering, Science and Research Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Engineering, Science and Research Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Reactor and Nuclear Safety Research School, Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute, Isfahan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Reactor and Nuclear Safety Research School
Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Reactor and Nuclear Safety School, Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute, 81465-1589, Isfahan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science & Engineering, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science & Engineering, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, titania-ceria-graphene quantum dot (TC-GQD) nanocomposite was synthesized by hydrothermal method for the first time. The prepared nanomaterials were characterized by XRD, FTIR dynamic light scattering (DLS), FESEM, HRTEM, and EDX spectroscopy along with elemental mapping. The synergistic effect of the nanocomposite components was studied by diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) and electrical conductivity meter. The results showed that band gap of TC-GQD nanocomposite was shifted to visible lights relative to its components (1.3 eV), and electrical conductivity of the sample was significant increased to 89.5 μS cm−1. After chemical and physical characterization, prepared new nanocomposites were used to design a new electrochemical (EC) and photoelectrochemical (PEC) dopamine (DA) sensors. In both EC and PEC methods effecting experimental parameters were optimized. Due to the synergic effect of the nanocomposite components, an outstanding photocurrent response was observed for DA based on PEC sensor. A linear calibration curve with a lower detection limit of 22 nM DA, and sensitivity of 13.8 mA/mM(DA), in a wider range of 0.3–750 μM DA, was obtained for TC-GQD/GCE electrode in PEC. While, the TC-GQD/GCE electrode detected DA in the range of 1–500 μM DA, with two linear calibration curve, detection limit of 0.22 μM DA, and sensitivity of 4.9 mA/mM(DA), in the EC. Observed results from EC and PEC sensors are presented and compared.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA), as an important neurotransmitter, has significant role in some diseases, schizophrenia, parkinson and depression (Shervedani et al., 2006). Therefore development of an accurate, rapid and user-friendly methods for the determination of DA is important. So far, owing to electroactivity of DA, electrochemical methods have been used due to low cost and high sensitivity detection. Two different methodologies were employed for electrochemical detection of DA. In the first methodology, electrochemical (EC) sensor based on nanostructured materials with electrocatalytic and specific property were used to electro-oxidation/reduction of DA (Bagherzadeh et al. 2013, 2015; Bagherzadeh and Heydari, 2013; Shervedani and Bagherzadeh, 2008; Shervedani et al. 2006, 2010). In the second methodology, photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor based on nanostructured semiconductor materials, with multi-step oxidation of DA process, chemical oxidation of DA by hydroxyl radicals and electrochemical oxidation on electrode surface, was developed (Alves et al., 2016; Azzouz et al., 2019; Cai et al., 2015; Hao et al., 2012; Hun et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019; Prado et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2019). High reproducibility baseline signal with low standard deviation and increased analytical signal, are two main advantages of the PEC in comparison to EC sensor due to on/off photocurrent responses and multi-step oxidation process (Prado et al., 2019). The mentioned advantages have led to more applicability of PEC sensors in portable devices for in-field monitoring and fast screening purposes.

Among the semiconductors, TiO2 is an important material due to its prominent properties, good chemical and physical stability, non-toxicity, high photoelectric activity, adequate light absorption, special energy levels and low cost (Ahmadi et al., 2014). However, due to the wide band gap of titania (≈3.2 eV)it shows photoelectric activity only within UV wavelengths (Ahmadi et al. 2014, 2018). Unfortunately the UV lights damage biomolecules and it's not applicable for designing a PEC sensors for biomolecules. In this regard, one of the ways to improve the photoelectric activity of titania within visible lights range, is doping of TiO2 with rare earth elements such as cerium (Štengl et al., 2009). When, TiO2 was doped with cerium oxide, the absorption edge of titania was shifted toward the red wavelengths and effectively reduce the recombination of the electron-hole pair by trapping the electrons (Ahmadi et al., 2014; Muñoz-Batista et al., 2014; Štengl et al., 2009).

Graphene is another material with unique property such as, high specific surface area, high electrical conductivity, high mobility of charge, and unique optical properties (Bagherzadeh and Farahbakhsh, 2015). The study has shown that graphene quantum dots (GQD) can be used for improvements of the electrical and optical properties (Bacon et al., 2014). The GQD represents the features of graphene and carbon quantum dots simultaneously. Due to its small size and its tunable band gap, the GQD has also photoluminescence and semiconductivity properties (Bacon et al., 2014; Güçlü et al., 2014). Furthermore, in comparison with other quantum dots, such as PbS and CdS, the GQD has much better stability and much less toxicity (Hashemzadeh et al., 2016).

In the present study, titania, ceria, and GQD were selected to prepare a new nanocomposites for the EC and PEC sensors. DA, as a biomolecule model, was selected for comparison of the EC and visible light PEC sensors performance, and the synergistic effects of the nanocomposite components and experimental conditions to reach a suitable DA responses were investigated.

Tetra-n-butyl orthotitanate (>98%), ethanol absolute, nitric acid (63%), hydrazine hydrate (80%) were purchased from Merk®. Cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (>99.5%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar®. Espresso coffee was purchased from Orbis®. The X-ray diffraction) XRD) patterns were obtained using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) at a scan rate of 0.05 2θs−1, (Philips PW3040). The particle size distribution of the powders was measured using dynamic light scattering (DLS; Vasco/Corduan technology). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum peak was recorded by using a FTIR spectrophotometer) Jasco; 6300 (Ultraviolet–visible light diffuse reflectance spectrums (UV–Vis DRS) were recorded by using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer) Jasco; V-670) within the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were carried out by fluorescence spectrophotometer (Scino FS-2) using excitation wavelength (λex) of 450 nm, and the emission spectrum was scanned over a wavelength range of 300–900 nm. The microstructure of the nanoparticles was investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy) FESEM; Tescan mira) and elemental analysis and mapping were done by energy dispersive X-ray) EDX) and mapping technique. High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images of the sample were carried out by using a HRTEM, FEI TECnai G20, instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

The GQDs were prepared via the hydrothermal method, from cleaned and dried, at 80 °C, espresso coffee wastes (Wang et al., 2016). At the first step, 0.3 g coffee wastes and 3 mL hydrazine hydrate were dissolved into 30 mL deionized water in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min. Then the obtained suspension was transferred to a 70 mL teflon-sealed autoclave container, and heated to 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the product was filtered through a 0.22 mm microporous membrane. Finally, the dark brown sediment was freeze dried for about 3 days and the resulted residue, prepared GQDs, collected and used in the next steps.

Titania-ceria (TC) nanocomposite was synthesized by sol-gel method (Ahmadi et al., 2018). The molar ratio of Ce to Ti in TC nanocomposite was 25%. First, 7 mL of tetra-n-butyl orthotitanate in 15 mL absolute ethanol, was added, dropwise under vigorous stirring to a 15 mL absolute ethanol containing 0.753 g of cerium nitrate hexahydrate for 2 h. Then, the mixture of nitric acid, deionized water and ethanol (0.05:1:1) were added dropwise to the previous solution until the sol was changed to gel. The gel was air dried at 80 °C. Finally, it was calcined at 750 °C for 2 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of titania-ceria-graphene quantum dot (TC-GQD) nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The TC-GQD nanocomposite was synthesized by hydrothermal method. The amount of GQD in the composite was 10%. First, GQD was dispersed in ethanol and deionized water along with ultrasonicated for 1 h. Then, the prepared TC nanocomposite was added to the obtained GQD suspension and stirred for 2 h. The final suspension was transferred to a teflon-sealed autoclave container and heated to 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, brown sediment was removed from the liquid with filter paper and dried at ambient temperature over several days, and brown TC-GQD nanocomposite powder was synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the EC and PEC electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: To construction the EC and PEC sensors, first, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished by using 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina powder, rinsed by deionized water, and sonicated in ethanol and deionized water bathes for 10 min 10 mg TC-GQD nanocomposite were dispersed in 5 mL ethanol and deionized water and then 10 μL of the suspension was drop coated onto the GCE surface and dried at room temperature to construction of the TC-GQD/GCE. The TC-GQD/GCE was rinsed with water several times prior to use. Applying the same method, TC/GCE and GQD/GCE were fabricated for comparison with the TC-GQD/GCE.

The EC and PEC measurements were performed using PARSTAT 2273 PGSTAT and recorded by a conventional tree-electrode system where the modified GCE, 3 mm in diameter, was used as working electrode, an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl solution) as a reference and Pt wire as a counter electrode. The electrochemical responses of the modified electrodes towards DA, ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) in 0.1 M PBS, under different conditions were evaluated by using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) techniques. In addition, the PEC currents were recorded in 0.1 M PBS at 0.0 V vs. OCP, under irradiation of a 360 W halogen lamp (GX 6.35 Osram®) within visible wavelength range.

XRD. The XRD patterns of the prepared samples in Fig. 1 a show that the TC and TC-GQD nanocomposites contain anatase and cerianite crystals, respectively, and display characteristic broad peak of (002) plane at 2θ = 23° for the GQDs. Furthermore, overlapping the (101) plane of anatase of TC nanocomposite and (002) plane of GQD at 2θ as 25° (Fig. 1b), led to a shift in observed peak for TC-GQD nanocomposite, in comparison with TC nanocomposite. This consequently confirms the presence of GQDs in the TC-GQD composition. There are similar reports in the literatures, as well (Ahmadi et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2016).

FTIR. Fig. 1c shows the FTIR spectrums of the prepared samples. It is obvious, the characteristic peaks of anatase and CeO bond of TC nanocomposite (Ahmad et al., 2017; Bai et al., 2014) as well as carboxyl, carbonyl and epoxy peaks of GQD (Huang et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2015) are present in the TC-GQD nanocomposite. Observed results confirmed successful preparation of TC-GQD nanocomposite.

DLS. Fig. 1d shows the DLS results of prepared nanomaterials in this work. As can be seen, the particle sizes of the prepared samples are in nanoscale size and the size distribution of synthesized GQD is considerably narrow.

DRS. To estimation the band gap of the prepared samples, DRS analysis and Kubelka-Munk function calculation were used and the results are presented in Fig. 1e and f, which are similar to the previous data (Ahmadi et al., 2018; López and Gómez, 2011). As can be seen from observed data, the absorption edge of the TC-GQD nanocomposite, completely shifted to the visible wavelength range and it is broadened in comparison with the other samples. The values of ≈1.4, ≈2.0, and ≈1.3 eV were estimated for the GQD, TC, and TC-GQD nanocomposites band gap, respectively. It is obvious, the observed band gap for TC-GQD nanocomposite (1.3 eV) is significantly lower than reported value for pure TiO2 as ≈3.2 eV (Ahmadi et al. 2014, 2018).

Conductivity. The electrical conductivities of the prepared nanomaterials were studied, and values of 11.9, 189, and 89.5 μS cm−1 were measured for TC, GQD, and TC-GQD samples, respectively. Significant enhancement in the electrical conductivity of the TC-GQD (89.5 μS cm−1) in comparison to TC (11.9 μS cm−1) nanocomposite is due to the presence of GQD in the composition.

PL. The PL spectrums of the prepared samples are presented in Fig. 1. g. As can be seen, all three samples exhibit blue, green and red emissions at 470 nm, 540 nm and 820 nm within visible light range, respectively. In addition, the PL emission of the TC-GQD nanocomposite was decreased significantly in comparison to the TC. The band gap of TC-GQD nanocomposite is smaller and consequently the number of photo excited electron-hole pair in it must be higher than the other. Meanwhile, the PL emission of this sample was decreased (Fig. 1. g spectrum of TC-GQD). Observed phenomenon may be due to the presence of high conductive GQD (189 μS cm−1) and trapping the excited electrons by its functional groups that caused to postponement of the recombination rate of photo-induced charge carriers (Gayathri et al., 2015; Thien et al., 2014).

FESEM. The morphology of TC-GQD nanocomposite was studied by using FESEM and the images are shown in Fig. 2 and b. As can be seen, the TC-GQD nanocomposite has uniform structure and its mean size is 17 ± 1 nm. The EDX spectrum and elemental composition of the nanocomposite powder is reported in Fig. 2c. The map analysis revealed that all elements were uniformly distributed throughout the TC-GQD nanocomposite (Fig. 2d). Observed results are in consist with previous data from XRD and confirmed successful preparation of the TC-GQD nanocomposite.

HRTEM. Fig. 2e–g shows the HRTEM images of TC-GQD nanocomposite. As can be seen from Fig. 2e, the GQDs are randomly dispersed in the composite matrix with irregular shapes. Additionally, Fig. 2f and g shows clear lattice fringes of graphene with an in-plane lattice parameter of approximately 0.35 nm that can be ascribed to (002) plane of GQD (Pan et al., 2010). Obtained results proved presence of the GQD nanosheets in the TC-GQD nanocomposite.

In order to show the synergistic effect of the nanocomposite components on the electrochemical behaviors of the TC-GQD, the electrochemical oxidation of DA was studied in the presence of constant concentration of AA (1 mM) and (100 μM) UA, by using the GQD, TC and TC-GQD modified GCE electrode (Fig. 3 ). It is well known that the oxidation peak potential of DA is similar to AA and UA, which causes overlapping in the peaks and difficulty in the DA detection. As can be seen from Fig. 3a, by addition of DA to a solution containing AA and UA, new separated peak was not observed in DPV response of the GCE electrode. In the case of the GQD/GCE, at the absence of DA two distinguish oxidation peaks at 50 and 310 mV appeared for AA and UA, respectively. As can be seen, a new oxidation peak was observed at 180 mV after addition of DA to the solution. However, the observed peak at 180 mV is sensitive to DA concentration up to 50 μM DA (Fig. 3b). The same behavior was observed at the TC/GCE electrode (Fig. 3c). As can be seen, three separate peaks at 180, 450 and 580 mV were observed for AA, DA, and UA, respectively only for low concentrations of DA. At high concentrations of DA, the oxidation peak of DA and UA overlapped. Eventually, for the TC-GQD/GCE electrode, three separate peaks at 180, 400 and 580 mV were observed for AA, DA, and UA, respectively. Observed more than 150 mV peak to peak separation for DA and UA at the TC-GQD/GCE electrode allow more sensitive detection of DA (Fig. 3d). The electron transfer mechanism at TC-GQD/GCE electrode in the presence of DA can present schematically as in Fig. 3e. DA oxidized to dopamine-o-quinon (DOQ (and two electrons released from this oxidation process (Mu et al., 2012; Schindler and Bechtold, 2019). It is noteworthy that, due to the special geometric conditions of titania, electron transfer to TC-GQD nanocomposite is easily done (Gao et al., 2015; Urdaneta et al., 2014). On the other hand, the high electrical conductivity of the nanocomposite (89.5 μS/cm), not only accelerates electron transfer, but also increases the tendency of DA for more oxidation.

Due to chemical structure of DA, its electrochemical oxidation involve proton concentration and consequently its oxidation potential and oxidation current is pH sensitive. The effect of solution pH on the electrochemical response of DA at the TC-GQD/GCE in 0.1 M PBS with different pH values (3–8) was studied (Fig. 4 a). The data indicates that with the increasing of solution pH from 3 to 8, the oxidation potential shifted toward more negative potentials and maximum oxidation current was recorded at pH 4.0.

To find the nature of electron transfer at the modified surface electrode, the effect of scan rate on the redox peak current of DA was investigated at different scan rates in 0.1 M PBS (pH 4) as shown in Fig. 4b. It is obvious that, by increasing the scan rate from 10 to 400 mV/s the DA oxidation and reduction currents were increased. Plotting the redox peak currents against the square root of scan rate (Fig. 4c) shows that the redox currents of DA increased linearly which indicated that the electrochemical oxidation occurring on the TC-GQD/GCE has a diffusion controlled process.

For electrochemical detection of DA in the presence of AA and UA, the DPV responses of TC-GQD/GCE electrode was recorded in the potential range of −0.2 to 0.8 V in PBS (pH 4.0) containing 1 mM AA and 100 μM UA and various concentration of DA from 0 to 1000 μM (Fig. 4e). Two linear calibration curves, from 1 to 50 μM and from 50 to 500 μM, were observed with correlation coefficients (r2) of 0.9929 and 0.9986, respectively. The detection limit of 0.22 μM (S/N = 3), was calculated for DA by using TC-GQD/GCE via EC methodology. The reproducibility of the TC-GQD/GCE was evaluated by five repetitive determinations (n = 5) for each concentration of DA. The results showed that relative standard deviation (RSD) was 5.5% for 250 μM DA. These results indicated the acceptable repeatability of the TC-GQD/GCE. Stability of the TC-GQD/GCE electrode towards DA after 20 days was checked and result shows only 9.7% drop, indicating its good stability.

The PEC responses of TC-GQD/GCE in the absence and presence of DA were investigated in 0.1 M PBS, pH: 7.4, with and without visible light and are shown in Fig. 5 a. The amount of photocurrent has been dramatically enhanced by the addition of DA, because light irradiation causes electrons transformed from valence band (VB) to conduction band (CB), and induces electron-hole pair in the nanocomposite which also recombines a remarkable part of charge carriers. The electrons then transfer to the electrode. Presence of the DA, as an electron donor, lead to occupy the holes in the nanocomposite valence band and prevent recombination of the electron-hole pair. On the other hand, it causes to increase the number of electrons transmitted during this process. In generally, the presence of DA increases the number of effective charge carriers and increase the photocurrent (Hun et al., 2017; Ye et al., 2018).

The PEC responses of other modified electrodes were recorded and compared with TC-GQD/GCE in Fig. 5b. Under visible light irradiation (at bias potential of 0.0 V vs. OCP) different photocurrent responses are observed for modified electrodes. The photocurrent generated by the GCE is approximately ignorable, and the increase of photocurrent response recorded by TC/GCE compared to T/GCE shows the beneficial synergistic effect of titania and ceria. TC nanocomposite has better photoelectric behavior than titania, and this behavior is related to the smaller band gap of TC nanocomposite (≈2.0 eV) in comparison to pure TiO2 (≈3.2 eV). Furthermore, the redox behavior of Ce ions leads to effective electron trapping and consequently increases the lifetime of the photoexcited electron-hole pair (Ahmadi et al. 2014, 2018), and photocurrent in TC/GCE. The GQD/GCE also exhibits photocurrent response, Fig. 5b. The observed behavior in GQD is due to excitation of electron-hole pair under irradiation of visible light in GQD (band gap as ≈ 1.4 eV), in addition to electron trapping and preventing the electron-hole pair recombination due to structural defects and surface functional groups on GQD. Furthermore, due to the high electrical conductivity of GQDs (189 μS/cm), the lifetime of the photoexcited electron-hole pair is high.

It is obvious from Fig. 5b that remarkable photocurrent response (5 order higher than the other electrodes) was observed for TC-GQD/GCE due to the beneficial synergistic effect of the nanocomposite components. The observed outstanding photo response is due to synergistic effects of; small band gap (1.3 eV), presence of Ce ions and functional groups of GQDs, and high electrical conductivity of the TC-GQD nanocomposite (89.5 μS/cm), that cause increasing in the number of photoexcited electron-hole pairs, trapping the electrons, and preventing photoexcited electron-hole recombination, respectively.

The PEC electron transfer mechanism at TC-GQD/GCE in the presence of DA is schematically presented in Fig. 5c. DA can be excited to DA+◦ under visible light irradiation (Hun et al., 2017; Ye et al., 2018), and the excited electrons of DA can be transferred to the TC-GQD nanocomposite in two route. In the first route (1), DA binds to titania planes via its –OH functional groups in the special geometrical condition, accelerates electron transfer, and leads to increasing the number of charge carriers. (Gao et al., 2015; Urdaneta et al., 2014). In the second rout (2), DA acts as an electron donor, occupies the holes of the TC-GQD nanocomposite valence band and prevents the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs (Gao et al., 2015; Hun et al., 2017; Ye et al., 2018). The electrons of DA and photoexcited electrons in nanocomposites are also transferred to the counter electrode and the recorded photocurrent by modified electrodes in the presence of DA is amplified compared to the unmodified electrode.

Effect of pH on the PEC response of the TC-GQD/GCE electrode in 0.1 M PBS containing 50 μM DA in pH range of 3–8, was investigated and is shown in Fig. 6 a. In the acidic pHs the interaction of DA with surface functional groups is improved and electron transfer from route (1) is dominant. As can be seen, at pH 4 the maximum photocurrent was observed and this pH was selected as optimized pH. The effect of the applied potential on the PEC response of TC-GQD/GCE electrode in 0.1 M PBS containing 50 μM DA was investigated and observed results presented in Fig. 6b. As can be seen, the photocurrent responses were increased by increasing the applied potential, at both darkness and irradiation conditions. In the darkness condition; by applying the potentials greater than the OCP value, the current response was increased due to capacitive and faradaic reactions at the surface electrode. Moreover, in the irradiation mode, accompanied capacitance and faradaic currents with PEC response lead to increase the observed responses. Ignoring the capacitive and faradaic currents in observed results, and avoiding interferences of other species in the real samples, the applied potential as 0.0 V vs. OCP was selected.

In the optimized conditions, the photocurrents of the TC-GQD/GCE towards DA concentrations in the range of 0–1000 μM were recorded (Fig. 6c). A liner calibration curve was obtained for PEC determination of DA within range of 0.3–750 μM DA, with correlation coefficient of 0.9966 (Fig. 6c inset). Furthermore, the detection limit of 0.022 μM DA (S/N = 3) and the RSD of 3.8% at 250 μM DA (n = 5) were observed for DA detection by using TC-GQD/GCE electrode in PEC strategy. The reproducibility of the PEC electrode is also evaluated by using five independent TC-GQD/GCE electrode toward 250 μM DA. Analyzing the photocurrent responses, the RSD value of 4.2% is achieved. These results indicated an acceptable reproducibility and repeatability of the TC-GQD/GCE. Our finding shows, only 8.5% drop was observed for the TC-GQD/GCE electrode responses after 20 days, indicating its high stability.

The interference of a few common interferes on the PEC response of TC-GQD/GCE electrode was investigated in the presence of 1.0 μM DA. AA in 1000-fold and UA in 100 fold of the DA concentration did not change the PEC response of the TC-GQD/GCE electrode. Furthermore, sucrose, glucose, Mg2+, Ca2+, and Na+ in 500-fold of the DA concentration did not change the signal of the TC-GQD/GCE electrode.

The performance of the PEC method was tested for determination of DA in dopamine hydrochloride injection samples. Pharmaceutical samples were prepared and adjusted to 5, 10 and 20 μM DA, in 0.1 M PBS, pH 7.4, by dilution of DOPADIC ampoule contents (5 mL containing 200 mg of DA hydrochloride in injection solution) and were tested five times, under optimal conditions. This method yielded an amount of 198 mg ± 0.4 in 5 mL, with a 99% (n = 5) recovery. More expanded applications were performed with other fixed, known concentrations as 5, 10 and 20 μM DA, and using the standard additions method. The PEC responses of the TC-GQD/GCE electrode were recorded and obtained results and their recovery values are illustrated in Table 1 . As can be concluded from data in Table 1, obtained results were in good agreement with the values of dopamine hydrochloride in diluted injection samples. These results of the accuracy for the determination of DA indicates the excellent recovery of the PEC method for the determinations in pharmaceutical preparations.
Finally, in comparison with the recent reports for DA detections, this work shows wider linear range response with low detection limit, especially in the PEC method (Table 2 ).

In this research, a TC-GQD nanocomposite was synthesized and characterized for the first time. Our results showed that the electrical conductivity and band gap of the TC-GQD nanocomposite dramatically improved, due to the synergistic effect of the nanocomposite components. The EC and PEC responses of the TC-GQD nanocomposite toward DA were investigated and compared. Based on our finding, the PEC strategy is preferable to EC in terms of detection speed, figure of merits, and stability of the electrode. For the EC determination strategy of DA, two linear calibration curve were observed within 1–500 μM DA with detection limit of 0.22 μM DA (S/N = 3), the RSD of 5.5% for repeatability (n = 5), and sensitivity of 4.9 mA/mM(DA) in high level concentration of DA. In comparison, PEC determination strategy of DA showed wider linear range of 0.3–750 μM with one linear calibration curve, the lower detection limit, 22 nM DA (S/N = 3), higher repeatability, RSD of 3.8% (n = 5), and more sensitivity, 13.8 mA/mM(DA). However the two strategy had the same stability in responses but due to avoid potential scanning in PEC method, the PEC is better than EC. Furthermore, our finding showed that the PEC method had potential to miniaturization in portable devices for in-field monitoring of DA, due to high sensitivity (2.8 times higher), low background signal for DA, low cost and simple preparation.

Nasrin Ahmadi: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Project administration, Writing - original draft. Mojtaba Bagherzadeh: Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Ali Nemati: Supervision, Validation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Islamic Azad University (Science and Research Branch) for providing facilities for this work.
